input: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?
debate_transcript: [Causal_Scientist]: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: This is an interesting question that touches on the nature of causation and the role of multiple sufficient causes. To determine if the department budget committee caused the approval of Prof. Smith's application, we need to consider the concept of 'INUS' conditions—insufficient but non-redundant parts of a condition which is itself unnecessary but sufficient for the occurrence of the effect. In this case, both committees are individually sufficient to cause the approval, but neither is necessary. So, while the department budget committee did contribute to the outcome, it's not the sole cause. The IT committee's approval alone would have sufficed. This raises ethical questions about responsibility and credit. If both committees had denied the application, would they share blame? Conversely, since both approved, do they share equal credit? It's a nuanced situation that challenges our traditional notions of cause and effect.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I'm not sure I understand all the big words you're using, but I think I get the general idea. So, you're saying that even though both committees approved the application, the IT committee alone could have done the job? That's like saying if I have two keys to my house, and I use both to open the door, but I could have used just one. Does that mean the second key didn't do anything? It still feels like it played a part, even if it wasn't absolutely necessary. So, in a way, the department budget committee did cause the approval, right? Or at least, they contributed to it. It's like when my wife and I both remind our kid to do his homework. Even if he would have done it if only one of us reminded him, it still feels like both reminders played a role. What do you think about that comparison? Does it make sense to think about it that way?

[Causal_Scientist]: That's a helpful analogy, Ordinary_Citizen. Using your key example, we can think of the committees as two separate keys that can both unlock the door (approve the application). The fact that either key alone can open the door doesn't negate the role of the second key. In causal terms, we might say that the department budget committee's approval was a contributing cause, even if it wasn't a necessary one. To put it another way, if we were to ask the counterfactual question—what if the department budget committee had denied the application?—the outcome would still be the same because the IT committee approved it. This suggests that the department budget committee's approval didn't change the outcome, but it's still part of the causal story. It's an interesting case of overdetermination, where multiple causes converge to produce the same effect. This makes me think about how we attribute causation in real-life scenarios where multiple factors are at play. How do we decide which causes to highlight and which to downplay?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I appreciate the key analogy, as it vividly illustrates the complexity of causal attribution. From an ethical perspective, the question of whether the department budget committee caused the approval is not just about the outcome but also about the intent, responsibility, and the process itself. Even if the IT committee's approval alone would have sufficed, the department budget committee's approval still played a role in the process. This raises questions about moral responsibility and credit assignment. Should both committees share equal credit for the approval, or should the IT committee receive more recognition due to its non-redundant role? Moreover, if we consider the potential for future scenarios where only one committee approves, how should we evaluate the fairness and justice of the approval process? It's a nuanced ethical landscape that intertwines with the causal analysis you've presented.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I see what you're both saying. It's like when I'm cooking dinner, and my wife helps with the sides. Even if the main dish alone would make a meal, her contributions still matter. But here's something I'm wondering: if the department budget committee is known for approving almost everything, does that change things? It's like if my wife only ever helps with the sides and never the main dish. Does her always saying 'yes' make her approval less meaningful? Or is it still important that she said 'yes' this time? Also, if the IT committee is the one that almost always approves, should they be the main ones responsible for making sure the applications are good? It seems like the department budget committee is just going through the motions. What do you think about that?

[Causal_Scientist]: That's an insightful point, Ordinary_Citizen. The predictability of the department budget committee's approval does add another layer to the causal analysis. In causal terms, we might consider the probability of the effect given the cause. If the department budget committee's approval is almost certain, it might not contribute much to the overall probability of the outcome. This is similar to how, in your cooking analogy, if your wife's help is always the same, it might not change the overall outcome much. However, it's important to note that even if the department budget committee's approval is predictable, it still plays a role in the causal chain. It's like a backup system—it might not often be needed, but it's still part of the process. Regarding responsibility, it's true that the IT committee might bear more responsibility for ensuring the quality of applications, given their more discerning role. This raises interesting questions about how we assign responsibility in systems with redundant or overlapping roles. How do we balance the contributions of different actors when some are more active or discerning than others?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: This discussion brings to mind the ethical concept of 'moral luck.' Even if the department budget committee's approval is predictable, it still contributes to the overall outcome, and thus, its members share some moral responsibility. The fact that they almost always approve doesn't absolve them of this responsibility, but it might mitigate it to some extent. It's akin to a jury that always finds the defendant not guilty—while they might not be directly responsible for any miscarriage of justice, their consistent pattern of decisions could still be seen as contributing to a flawed system. Regarding the IT committee's role, their more discerning nature does suggest a greater responsibility in ensuring the quality of applications. However, this also raises questions about fairness and equity. Should the IT committee bear more responsibility simply because they have more information or a more rigorous process? Or should the department budget committee also be held to a higher standard, given that their approval is part of the necessary process? It's a complex interplay of causal responsibility and ethical accountability.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I think I'm starting to understand this better. So, even if the department budget committee is like a backup system, they still have some responsibility. It's like how my wife might not change the main dish much, but she's still part of the meal. But what about when things go wrong? If an application gets approved that shouldn't have, who's to blame? Is it just the IT committee because they're supposed to be more careful? Or does the department budget committee share some blame too, even if they always say yes? It's like if my wife never checks the sides I'm making and something goes wrong, is she partly to blame even if she usually doesn't help much? I'm not sure about this, but it's interesting to think about.

[Causal_Scientist]: You're touching on a crucial aspect of causal responsibility: blame assignment in cases of failure. In this scenario, if an application gets approved that shouldn't have, we can consider the counterfactual situation where one or both committees denied the application. If the IT committee alone could have denied the application, they might bear more responsibility for the failure. However, the department budget committee still plays a role in the process, and their approval, even if predictable, contributes to the outcome. This is similar to how, in your cooking analogy, if something goes wrong with the sides, your wife might share some blame, even if she usually doesn't help much. It's a matter of degree, though. The IT committee, with its more discerning role, likely bears more responsibility, but the department budget committee isn't entirely off the hook. This raises interesting questions about how we assign blame in systems with overlapping responsibilities. How do we determine the appropriate level of blame for each committee when things go wrong?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: This is a profound exploration of moral responsibility in complex systems. When considering blame assignment, we must also account for the intent and awareness of each committee. If the department budget committee is aware of its tendency to approve applications and does nothing to change this pattern, their inaction could be seen as a form of negligence. This would strengthen their moral responsibility in cases of failure. On the other hand, if the IT committee is aware of the department budget committee's predictable behavior and relies on it, they might also share some blame for not ensuring a more robust approval process. It's a matter of both individual and collective responsibility. To further complicate things, we might consider the ethical implications of the committees' behaviors. Is the department budget committee's consistent approval a form of moral laziness? Is the IT committee's more discerning approach a form of moral vigilance? These questions invite us to delve deeper into the ethical dimensions of causal responsibility.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: This is getting pretty complicated. I think I need to step back and think about this in simpler terms. Let me try to summarize what I understand: both committees are involved, but one is more careful than the other. If something goes wrong, the more careful one is more to blame, but the other one isn't completely innocent. It's like if I'm driving and my wife is in the passenger seat. If we get into an accident, I'm more to blame because I'm driving, but if my wife saw something dangerous and didn't say anything, she might share some blame too. But I'm not sure how to apply this to the committees. Maybe the IT committee is like the driver, and the department budget committee is like the passenger. But what if the passenger is always asleep? Does that change things? I think I need to think about this more, but I have to go now. Maybe we can talk about this later.
